user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, seeking refund with interest and costs.

missing_fields: Specific cause of action accrual date
assumptions: Jurisdiction lies in the District Court based on suit value or cause of action

The plea of set-off has to be raised specifically in the written statement and the plaintiff is expected to file written

raised the plea of set-off in the written statement nor paid court-fee thereon, the claim of amount allegedly incurred 
by the defendants cannot be treated as a claim for set-off.9

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the advance amount was transferred from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor returned the advance amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

examples.bad: Under Section 65, the Defendant is bound to restore the amount.
examples.good: Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, the business transaction between the parties has resulted in a total failure of consideration inasmuch as the Defendant received Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- but provided no benefit whatsoever to the Plaintiff.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations under the business transaction despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim refund of the advance amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding refund, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.text:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Between the Primary Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and the Opposite Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, this suit is instituted in the District Court. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue; the Defendant is sui juris and competent to be sued.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff submits that this Hon'ble Court, District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, within the State of {{STATE}}, has territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain the present suit. Territorial jurisdiction is grounded on the Defendant's address at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the local limits of this Court, and on the fact that Rs. 20,00,000 was paid on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by the Plaintiff from the Plaintiff's account maintained with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}}, located within the same local limits. The agreement between the parties was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}, which is also within the jurisdiction of this Court. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_PLACE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performance, resulting in continuing breach and a total failure of consideration, thereby tying the dispute to this Court's territorial domain. Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the suit seeks Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court for a money-recovery claim. The Court is properly vested with the authority to adjudicate the suit on the dates and places stated above, within the local limits of {{COURT_PLACE}} in {{STATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came to know each other through a mutual business contact in {{PLACE_NAME}}, and after preliminary discussions in person and by correspondence the parties progressed to formal negotiations in relation to a proposed business transaction. The discussions culminated in a written agreement where the terms and conditions of the proposed arrangement were recorded, and Annexure A contains the contract document evidencing this relationship and the scope of the contemplated venture.

2. On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the parties executed a written agreement (Annexure A) under which the Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance of Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant for pursuing the stated business transaction. The terms of the agreement set out the purpose for which the funds were to be used and the overall framework governing the venture, including the Defendant’s obligation to perform the agreed transaction in accordance with the contemplated plan and scope set out in Annexure A.

3. In pursuance of the agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by bank transfer to the Defendant’s account, and the payment proof is reflected in Annexure B. The payment was made as consideration for the Defendant to initiate and pursue the business transaction in accordance with the terms of Annexure A, as recorded in the transaction records and bank records annexed at Annexure B.

4. The Defendant undertook to perform the agreed transaction and to bring about the completion of the venture by the date contemplated in the contract. The obligations on the Defendant included using the advance for the specified venture and carrying out the actions required to finalize the transaction in accordance with the scope described in Annexure A, with {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} referenced as the date by which performance was expected to be completed.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant had not commenced the performance nor completed the transaction for which the Plaintiff had advanced Rs. 20,00,000, nor had the Defendant refunded the amount or otherwise complied with the contractual arrangement. The Plaintiff repeatedly sought status updates and fulfillment of the Defendant’s obligations, but no substantive steps toward performance or refund were taken, and there was no issue of funds reverting to the Plaintiff as contemplated by the agreement.

6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}} the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding the refund of Rs. 20,00,000 together with interest and costs, with a copy furnished to the Defendant as required. The notice is recorded as Annexure C. A response to the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} is reflected in Annexure D. Thereafter, the Plaintiff instituted this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the advance amount along with interest and costs, and asserting that the cause of action first accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and has a continuing character until the relief sought is granted. Annexure F contains any further supporting documents relied upon in establishing the factual basis for the claim.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates an obligation to restore to the party from whom money was received any advantage obtained under a contract where the contract is void or the consideration fails; the triggering conditions are (i) there exists an agreement under which the Plaintiff paid money as consideration for a proposed transaction; (ii) the transaction fails due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in total loss of consideration; (iii) the Plaintiff has not received any value or benefit in return. In the present case the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} at {{PLACE_OF_PAYMENT}} as an advance for a business transaction, which failed due to the Defendant’s default on or about {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}; the Plaintiff has received no benefit from the transaction, and no valid adjustment or set-off exists to justify retention of the amount. Therefore, the Defendant is obligated to restore Rs. 20,00,000 to the Plaintiff, along with interest and costs, as restitution for the total failure of consideration; there is no requirement of a forfeiture clause or any other condition precedent to the application of Section 65, and no counter-claim is asserted by the Defendant that would extinguish the right to restitution. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act, which governs suits for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, provides that the accrual of the cause of action occurs on the date of failure, namely {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}, and the suit is within the three-year period from that date; the Plaintiff has filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, and if the Court finds a different accrual date, the continuing nature of the cause of action ensures timely relief against the Defendant. The subject-matter value is Rs. 20,00,000, and the Plaintiff seeks refund of that amount together with interest at the statutory rate and costs; court fee shall be payable as per the applicable District Court schedule; Annexure A contains the Agreement, Annexure B the payment proof, Annexure C the legal notice, Annexure D any reply, Annexure E acknowledgment/admission (if any), and Annexure F additional documents; The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant remains in default and no refund has been provided; there is no part performance to be argued, no forfeiture clause to undermine restitution, and no counter-claim by the Defendant; the Plaintiff prays for restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs and any other relief the Court may deem fit. Verification of this pleading is attached herein as required by law, with the Plaintiff asserting the truthfulness of the averments and the facts stated herein.

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, respectfully submits this suit before the District Court seeking refund of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as an advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant’s default, and prays for such other and further relief as may be appropriate.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action in the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Rs. 20,00,000 advance and to perform the business transaction that was the subject of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} entered into between Plaintiff and Defendant at {{PLACE_NAME}}, Plaintiff having paid the amount on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in reliance on the Defendant’s promise to complete the transaction by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}; the essential consideration for the contract totally failed on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} due to Defendant’s default, which triggered the Plaintiff’s right to restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, the rule being that where the consideration for a contract totally fails, the promisor must return the value conveyed to the promisee and the promisee is entitled to sue for recovery of the money advanced; in the present case the Plaintiff is thus entitled to recover Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding refund of the Rs. 20,00,000 and interest and costs, and despite service of the notice the Defendant did not comply or repay, thereby sustaining the right to sue and continuing the cause of action. The cause of action is continuing because the Defendant continues to retain the Rs. 20,00,000 advanced on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} without refund, and has not performed or compensated the Plaintiff as of {{FILING_DATE}}; this wrongful retention forms the basis of the suit for refund, with interest and costs as prayed. The value of the suit is Rs. 20,00,000; court fee payable is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}; the Plaintiff also seeks interest as provided by law and costs. IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

Between the Primary Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and the Opposite Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, this suit is instituted in the District Court. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue; the Defendant is sui juris and competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

The Plaintiff submits that this Hon'ble Court, District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, within the State of {{STATE}}, has territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain the present suit. Territorial jurisdiction is grounded on the Defendant's address at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the local limits of this Court, and on the fact that Rs. 20,00,000 was paid on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by the Plaintiff from the Plaintiff's account maintained with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}}, located within the same local limits. The agreement between the parties was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}, which is also within the jurisdiction of this Court. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_PLACE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performance, resulting in continuing breach and a total failure of consideration, thereby tying the dispute to this Court's territorial domain. Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the suit seeks Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court for a money-recovery claim. The Court is properly vested with the authority to adjudicate the suit on the dates and places stated above, within the local limits of {{COURT_PLACE}} in {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came to know each other through a mutual business contact in {{PLACE_NAME}}, and after preliminary discussions in person and by correspondence the parties progressed to formal negotiations in relation to a proposed business transaction. The discussions culminated in a written agreement where the terms and conditions of the proposed arrangement were recorded, and Annexure A contains the contract document evidencing this relationship and the scope of the contemplated venture.

2. On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the parties executed a written agreement (Annexure A) under which the Plaintiff agreed to provide an advance of Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant for pursuing the stated business transaction. The terms of the agreement set out the purpose for which the funds were to be used and the overall framework governing the venture, including the Defendant’s obligation to perform the agreed transaction in accordance with the contemplated plan and scope set out in Annexure A.

3. In pursuance of the agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} by bank transfer to the Defendant’s account, and the payment proof is reflected in Annexure B. The payment was made as consideration for the Defendant to initiate and pursue the business transaction in accordance with the terms of Annexure A, as recorded in the transaction records and bank records annexed at Annexure B.

4. The Defendant undertook to perform the agreed transaction and to bring about the completion of the venture by the date contemplated in the contract. The obligations on the Defendant included using the advance for the specified venture and carrying out the actions required to finalize the transaction in accordance with the scope described in Annexure A, with {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} referenced as the date by which performance was expected to be completed.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant had not commenced the performance nor completed the transaction for which the Plaintiff had advanced Rs. 20,00,000, nor had the Defendant refunded the amount or otherwise complied with the contractual arrangement. The Plaintiff repeatedly sought status updates and fulfillment of the Defendant’s obligations, but no substantive steps toward performance or refund were taken, and there was no issue of funds reverting to the Plaintiff as contemplated by the agreement.

6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}} the Plaintiff issued a legal notice demanding the refund of Rs. 20,00,000 together with interest and costs, with a copy furnished to the Defendant as required. The notice is recorded as Annexure C. A response to the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} is reflected in Annexure D. Thereafter, the Plaintiff instituted this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the advance amount along with interest and costs, and asserting that the cause of action first accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and has a continuing character until the relief sought is granted. Annexure F contains any further supporting documents relied upon in establishing the factual basis for the claim.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates an obligation to restore to the party from whom money was received any advantage obtained under a contract where the contract is void or the consideration fails; the triggering conditions are (i) there exists an agreement under which the Plaintiff paid money as consideration for a proposed transaction; (ii) the transaction fails due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in total loss of consideration; (iii) the Plaintiff has not received any value or benefit in return. In the present case the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} at {{PLACE_OF_PAYMENT}} as an advance for a business transaction, which failed due to the Defendant’s default on or about {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}; the Plaintiff has received no benefit from the transaction, and no valid adjustment or set-off exists to justify retention of the amount. Therefore, the Defendant is obligated to restore Rs. 20,00,000 to the Plaintiff, along with interest and costs, as restitution for the total failure of consideration; there is no requirement of a forfeiture clause or any other condition precedent to the application of Section 65, and no counter-claim is asserted by the Defendant that would extinguish the right to restitution. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act, which governs suits for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, provides that the accrual of the cause of action occurs on the date of failure, namely {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}, and the suit is within the three-year period from that date; the Plaintiff has filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, and if the Court finds a different accrual date, the continuing nature of the cause of action ensures timely relief against the Defendant. The subject-matter value is Rs. 20,00,000, and the Plaintiff seeks refund of that amount together with interest at the statutory rate and costs; court fee shall be payable as per the applicable District Court schedule; Annexure A contains the Agreement, Annexure B the payment proof, Annexure C the legal notice, Annexure D any reply, Annexure E acknowledgment/admission (if any), and Annexure F additional documents; The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant remains in default and no refund has been provided; there is no part performance to be argued, no forfeiture clause to undermine restitution, and no counter-claim by the Defendant; the Plaintiff prays for restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs and any other relief the Court may deem fit. Verification of this pleading is attached herein as required by law, with the Plaintiff asserting the truthfulness of the averments and the facts stated herein.

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, respectfully submits this suit before the District Court seeking refund of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as an advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant’s default, and prays for such other and further relief as may be appropriate.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action in the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the Rs. 20,00,000 advance and to perform the business transaction that was the subject of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} entered into between Plaintiff and Defendant at {{PLACE_NAME}}, Plaintiff having paid the amount on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in reliance on the Defendant’s promise to complete the transaction by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}; the essential consideration for the contract totally failed on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} due to Defendant’s default, which triggered the Plaintiff’s right to restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, the rule being that where the consideration for a contract totally fails, the promisor must return the value conveyed to the promisee and the promisee is entitled to sue for recovery of the money advanced; in the present case the Plaintiff is thus entitled to recover Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding refund of the Rs. 20,00,000 and interest and costs, and despite service of the notice the Defendant did not comply or repay, thereby sustaining the right to sue and continuing the cause of action. The cause of action is continuing because the Defendant continues to retain the Rs. 20,00,000 advanced on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} without refund, and has not performed or compensated the Plaintiff as of {{FILING_DATE}}; this wrongful retention forms the basis of the suit for refund, with interest and costs as prayed. The value of the suit is Rs. 20,00,000; court fee payable is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}; the Plaintiff also seeks interest as provided by law and costs. Verification: I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Plaintiff herein, do hereby verify that the contents of this Cause of Action are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief and that I have not filed any other suit in respect of the same cause of action. The suit is filed before the District Court having jurisdiction at {{PLACE_NAME}}.


LIMITATION

The Plaintiff asserts that the present claim for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant in relation to a business transaction is governed by Article 47 of the First Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing the obligations under the agreement, thereby causing total failure of consideration for the Rs. 20,00,000 advance paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in relation to the business transaction between Plaintiff and Defendant. The Plaintiff has instituted this suit on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within the three-year period from accrual, and is thus within time.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000, which equals the claim amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant for the business transaction that subsequently failed due to the Defendant's default. The court fee payable on the plaint is calculated under the applicable State Court Fees Act and is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

Pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 20,00,000 shall accrue from the date of default by the Defendant, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, until the date of filing of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, at the rate of 12% per annum. The pre-suit accrual is justified as it compensates for the use of the Plaintiff’s funds during the interim and aligns with commercial expectations in a money claim arising from a failed business transaction.

Pendente lite interest shall accrue on the same Rs. 20,00,000 from the date of institution of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, up to the date of decree, i.e., {{DECREED_DATE}}, at the rate of 12% per annum, as permitted under Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. The Court may award such interest to reflect the delay in disposition of the money claim and to compensate the Plaintiff for the use of its funds during litigation, in keeping with the commercial character of the transaction and the Defendant’s continued and unjustifiable non-payment. The factual basis for pendente lite interest rests on the Defendant’s ongoing withholding of the advance amount since {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the Plaintiff’s decision to pursue judicial relief by filing on {{FILING_DATE}}; Section 34 CPC governs this period and authorizes the Court to award interest on sums due from the date of filing until the date of decree.

Post-decree, until realization, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on the decretal amount at 12% per annum from the date of the decree, i.e., {{DECREED_DATE}}, until realization. This rate is justified by the commercial nature of the underlying transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, and the need to compensate the Plaintiff for the time value of money during post-decree enforcement. The legal basis for such post-decree interest is the general principle under Section 34 CPC that the Court may award interest on money decreed from the date of decree until payment, reflecting the loss of use and the cost of recovery in commercial disputes. The Plaintiff thus requests the court to fix future interest at 12% per annum on the decretal amount from {{DECREED_DATE}} until realization, consistent with the foregoing rationale and in light of the overall relief sought in the suit.


PRAYER

PRAYER

(a) A decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. 20,00,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant as advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant’s default, with costs of the suit and with interest on the principal amount as specified in sub-clauses (b) to (d) below; the value of the suit for the purpose of Court fee and jurisdiction is Rs. 20,00,000.

(b) Pre-suit interest on Rs. 20,00,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on account of the total failure of consideration and in consequence of the Defendant’s default, to be calculated and payable along with the principal amount.

(c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on Rs. 20,00,000 at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}}% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, to be paid by the Defendant to the Plaintiff until realization.

(d) Post-decree interest on Rs. 20,00,000 at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until actual realisation, to be paid by the Defendant to the Plaintiff.

(e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice to the Defendant dated {{NOTICE_DATE}}, amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}; the breakdown of costs is Rs. {{COURT_FEE}} as court fee and Rs. {{NOTICE_COST}} as pre-litigation notice costs, with the Plaintiff claiming the total as stated.

(f) Any other relief that the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including any ancillary or consequential relief arising from the above, as to which the Plaintiff is entitled in law.

Note: The Plaintiff prays for such other and further relief as this Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates an obligation to restore to the party from whom money was received any advantage obtained under a contract where the contract is void or the consideration fails; the triggering conditions are (i) there exists an agreement under which the Plaintiff paid money as consideration for a proposed transaction; (ii) the transaction fails due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in total loss of consideration; (iii) the Plaintiff has not received any value or benefit in return. In the present case the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} at {{PLACE_OF_PAYMENT}} as an advance for a business transaction, which failed due to the Defendant’s default on or about {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}; the Plaintiff has received no benefit from the transaction, and no valid adjustment or set-off exists to justify retention of the amount. Therefore, the Defendant is obligated to restore Rs. 20,00,000 to the Plaintiff, along with interest and costs, as restitution for the total failure of consideration; there is no requirement of a forfeiture clause or any other condition precedent to the application of Section 65, and no counter-claim is asserted by the Defendant that would extinguish the right to restitution. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act, which governs suits for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, provides that the accrual of the cause of action occurs on the date of failure, namely {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}, and the suit is within the three-year period from that date; the Plaintiff has filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, and if the Court finds a different accrual date, the continuing nature of the cause of action ensures timely relief against the Defendant. The subject-matter value is Rs. 20,00,000, and the Plaintiff seeks refund of that amount together with interest at the statutory rate and costs; court fee shall be payable as per the applicable District Court schedule; Annexure A contains the Agreement, Annexure B the payment proof, Annexure C the legal notice, Annexure D any reply, Annexure E acknowledgment/admission (if any), and Annexure F additional documents; The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant remains in default and no refund has been provided; there is no part performance to be argued, no forfeiture clause to undermine restitution, and no counter-claim by the Defendant; the Plaintiff prays for restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs and any other relief the Court may deem fit. INTEREST

Pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 20,00,000 shall accrue from the date of default by the Defendant, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, until the date of filing of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, at the rate of 12% per annum. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance for a business transaction pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the purchase of goods or performance of services contemplated thereby to be completed by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Defendant, being obligated to perform or refund the amount by that date, defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to either perform the agreed obligation or refund the amount, resulting in wrongful retention of funds. The pre-suit accrual is justified as it compensates for the use of the Plaintiff’s funds during the interim and aligns with commercial expectations in a money claim arising from a failed business transaction.

Pendente lite interest shall accrue on the same Rs. 20,00,000 from the date of institution of the suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, up to the date of decree, i.e., {{DECREED_DATE}}, at the rate of 12% per annum, as permitted under Section 34 of the Civil Procedure Code. The Court may award such interest to reflect the delay in disposition of the money claim and to compensate the Plaintiff for the use of its funds during litigation, in keeping with the commercial character of the transaction and the Defendant’s continued and unjustifiable non-payment. The factual basis for pendente lite interest rests on the Defendant’s ongoing withholding of the advance amount since {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and the Plaintiff’s decision to pursue judicial relief by filing on {{FILING_DATE}}; Section 34 CPC governs this period and authorizes the Court to award interest on sums due from the date of filing until the date of decree.

Post-decree, until realization, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on the decretal amount at 12% per annum from the date of the decree, i.e., {{DECREED_DATE}}, until realization. This rate is justified by the commercial nature of the underlying transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds, and the need to compensate the Plaintiff for the time value of money during post-decree enforcement. The legal basis for such post-decree interest is the general principle under Section 34 CPC that the Court may award interest on money decreed from the date of decree until payment, reflecting the loss of use and the cost of recovery in commercial disputes. The Plaintiff thus requests the court to fix future interest at 12% per annum on the decretal amount from {{DECREED_DATE}} until realization, consistent with the foregoing rationale and in light of the overall relief sought in the suit.
